text:
icon  
o the man who loves art for its own sake,' remarked Sherlock Holmes, tossing aside the advertisement sheet of the Daily Telegraph , 'it is frequently in its least important  
and lowliest manifestations that the keenest pleasure is to be derived. It is pleasant to me to observe, Watson, that you have so far grasped this truth that in these little records of our cases which you have been good enough to draw up, and, I am bound to say, occasionally to embellish, you have given prominence not so much to the many causes c´ el` ebres and sensational trials in which I have figured but rather to those incidents which may have been trivial in themselves, but which have given room for those faculties of deduction and of logical synthesis which I have made my special province.'  
'And yet,' said I, smiling, 'I cannot quite hold myself absolved from the charge of sensationalism which has been urged against my records.'  
'You have erred, perhaps,' he observed, taking up a glowing cinder with the tongs and lighting with it the long cherry-wood pipe which was wont to replace his clay when he was in a disputatious rather than a meditative mood-'you have erred perhaps in attempting to put colour and life into each of your statements instead of confining yourself to the task of placing upon record that severe reasoning from cause to effect which is really the only notable feature about the thing.'  
'It seems to me that I have done you full justice in the matter,' I remarked with some coldness, for I was repelled by the egotism which I had more than once observed to be a strong factor in my friend's singular character.  
'No, it is not selfishness or conceit,' said he, answering, as was his wont, my thoughts rather than my words. 'If I claim full justice for my art, it is because it is an impersonal thing-a thing beyond myself. Crime is common. Logic is rare. Therefore it is upon the logic rather than upon the crime that you should dwell. You have degraded what should have been a course of lectures into a series of tales.'  
It was a cold morning of the early spring, and we sat after breakfast on either side of a cheery fire in the old room at Baker Street. A thick fog rolled down between the lines of dun-coloured houses, and the opposing windows loomed like dark, shapeless blurs through the heavy yellow wreaths. Our gas was lit and shone on the white cloth and glimmer of china and metal, for the table had not been cleared yet. Sherlock Holmes had been silent all the morning, dipping continuously into the advertisement columns of a succession of papers until at last, having apparently given up his search, he had emerged in no very sweet temper to lecture me upon my literary shortcomings.  
'At the same time,' he remarked after a pause, during which he had sat puffing at his long pipe and gazing down into the fire, 'you can hardly be open to a charge of sensationalism, for out of these cases which you have been so kind as to interest yourself in, a fair proportion do not treat of crime, in its legal sense, at all. The small matter in which I endeavoured to help the King of Bohemia, the singular experience of Miss Mary Sutherland, the problem connected with the man with the twisted lip, and the incident of the noble bachelor, were all matters which are outside the pale of the law. But in avoiding the sensational, I fear that you may have bordered on the trivial.'  
'The end may have been so,' I answered, 'but the methods I hold to have been novel and of interest.'  
'Pshaw, my dear fellow, what do the public, the great unobservant public, who could hardly tell a weaver by his tooth or a compositor by his left thumb, care about the finer shades of analysis and deduction! But, indeed, if you are trivial, I cannot blame you, for the days of the great cases are past. Man, or at least criminal man, has lost all enterprise and originality. As to my own little practice, it seems to be degenerating into an agency for recovering lost lead pencils and giving advice to young ladies from boarding-schools. I think that I have touched bottom at last, however. This note I had this morning marks my zero-point, I fancy. Read it!' He tossed a crumpled letter across to me.  
It was dated from Montague Place upon the preceding evening, and ran thus:  
D ear M r . H olmes :  
I am very anxious to consult you as to whether I should or should not accept a situation which has been offered to me as governess. I shall call at half-past ten to-morrow if I do not inconvenience you.  
-Yours faithfully,  
V iolet H unter .  
'Do you know the young lady?' I asked.  
'Not I.'  
'It is half-past ten now.'  
'Yes, and I have no doubt that is her ring.'  
'It may turn out to be of more interest than you think. You remember that the affair of the blue carbuncle, which appeared to be a mere whim at first, developed into a serious investigation. It may be so in this case, also.'  
'Well, let us hope so. But our doubts will very soon be solved, for here, unless I am much mistaken, is the person in question.'  
As he spoke the door opened and a young lady entered the room. She was plainly but neatly dressed, with a bright, quick face, freckled like a plover's egg, and with the brisk manner of a woman who has had her own way to make in the world.  
'You will excuse my troubling you, I am sure,' said she, as my companion rose to greet her, 'but I have had a very strange experience, and as I have no parents or relations of any sort from whom I could ask advice, I thought that perhaps you would be kind enough to tell me what I should do.'  
'Pray take a seat, Miss Hunter. I shall be happy to do anything that I can to serve you.'  
I could see that Holmes was favourably impressed by the manner and speech of his new client. He looked her over in his searching fashion, and then composed himself, with his lids drooping and his finger-tips together, to listen to her story.  
'I have been a governess for five years,' said she, 'in the family of Colonel Spence Munro, but two months ago the colonel received an appointment at Halifax, in Nova Scotia, and took his children over to America with him, so that I found myself without a situation. I advertised, and I answered advertisements, but without success. At last the little money which I had saved began to run short, and I was at my wit's end as to what I should do.  
'There is a well-known agency for governesses in the West End called Westaway's, and there I used to call about once a week in order to see whether anything had turned up which might suit me. Westaway was the name of the founder of the business, but it is really managed by Miss Stoper. She sits in her own little office, and the ladies who are seeking employment wait in an anteroom, and are then shown in one by one, when she consults her ledgers and sees whether she has anything which would suit them.  
'Well, when I called last week I was shown into the little office as usual, but I found that Miss Stoper was not alone. A prodigiously stout man with a very smiling face and a great heavy chin which rolled down in fold upon fold over his throat sat at her elbow with a pair of glasses on his nose, looking very earnestly at the ladies who entered. As I came in he gave quite a jump in his chair and turned quickly to Miss Stoper.
name: Chunk 1/8

raw_entities.name: Sherlock Holmes
raw_entities.description: A consulting detective who values logical deduction and impersonal logic over sensational crime narratives. He engages in a philosophical debate with Watson about the nature of his work and his client records, ultimately demonstrating his deductive prowess by anticipating the arrival of his next client.
context: 'o the man who loves art for its own sake,' remarked Sherlock Holmes, tossing aside the advertisement sheet of the Daily Telegraph , 'it is frequently in its least important and lowliest manifestations that the keenest pleasure is to be derived.'
'Crime is common. Logic is rare. Therefore it is upon the logic rather than upon the crime that you should dwell.'
'You remember that the affair of the blue carbuncle, which appeared to be a mere whim at first, developed into a serious investigation.'

raw_entities.description: The narrator and chronicler of Holmes's cases, who defends his literary style against Holmes's criticism of sensationalism. He serves as a foil to Holmes's cold logic, occasionally feeling repelled by his friend's egotism while ultimately deferring to his deductive methods.
context: 'I cannot quite hold myself absolved from the charge of sensationalism which has been urged against my records.'

'It seems to me that I have done you full justice in the matter,' I remarked with some coldness, for I was repelled by the egotism which I had more than once observed to be a strong factor in my friend's singular character.
'Well, let us hope so. But our doubts will very soon be solved, for here, unless I am much mistaken, is the person in question.'

'Pshaw, my dear fellow, what do the public, the great unobservant public, who could hardly tell a weaver by his tooth or a compositor by his left thumb, care about the finer shades of analysis and deduction!'

entities.name: Art for its own sake
entities.description: A philosophical stance introduced by Holmes to frame his approach to deduction. He equates his logical methods with an aesthetic pursuit, suggesting that true pleasure and value are found in the least important manifestations rather than grand spectacles.
context: 'o the man who loves art for its own sake,' remarked Sherlock Holmes, tossing aside the advertisement sheet of the Daily Telegraph , 'it is frequently in its least important and lowliest manifestations that the keenest pleasure is to be derived.'
'If I claim full justice for my art, it is because it is an impersonal thing-a thing beyond myself.'

entities.name: Zero-point
entities.description: Holmes's metaphorical designation for the absolute bottom of his professional decline, marked by the receipt of Violet Hunter's trivial consultation. It ironically precedes the discovery that the case may hold unexpected depth, subverting his own pessimistic assessment.
context: 'I think that I have touched bottom at last, however. This note I had this morning marks my zero-point, I fancy. Read it!'

properties.narrative_arc: feels his practice has degenerated to trivial matters, marking a 'zero-point' before Violet Hunter's arrival

financially strained after losing previous employment

relationships.description: Holmes and Watson collaborate as consulting detectives and share a residence, with Watson chronicling their cases.
relationships.context: I am bound to say, occasionally to embellish, you have given prominence not so much to the many causes c´ el` ebres and sensational trials in which I have figured but rather to those incidents which may have been trivial in themselves, but which have given room for those faculties of deduction and of logical synthesis which I have made my special province.

relationships.description: Holmes critiques Watson's literary style, arguing it prioritizes sensationalism over strict logical reasoning.

properties.quote: You have erred perhaps in attempting to put colour and life into each of your statements instead of confining yourself to the task of placing upon record that severe reasoning from cause to effect

relationships.context: You have erred perhaps in attempting to put colour and life into each of your statements instead of confining yourself to the task of placing upon record that severe reasoning from cause to effect which is really the only notable feature about the thing.

relationships.description: Holmes meets Violet Hunter as a new client seeking professional advice on an employment offer.
relationships.context: I could see that Holmes was favourably impressed by the manner and speech of his new client.

relationships.description: Holmes offers to consult with Violet Hunter regarding her dilemma about accepting a governess position.
relationships.context: I shall be happy to do anything that I can to serve you.

relationships.description: Holmes forms a positive impression of Violet Hunter's character, manner, and speech upon meeting her.

properties.quote: favourably impressed by the manner and speech of his new client

relationships.context: I could see that Holmes was favourably impressed by the manner and speech of his new client.

relationships.description: Holmes previously helped the King of Bohemia with a delicate matter that fell outside legal boundaries.
relationships.context: The small matter in which I endeavoured to help the King of Bohemia...

relationships.description: Holmes investigated the singular and unusual experience involving Miss Mary Sutherland.
relationships.context: the singular experience of Miss Mary Sutherland...

relationships.description: Holmes observes and prepares to interrogate Violet Hunter upon her arrival at his residence.

properties.contribution: greets her and prepares to listen to her story

relationships.context: As he spoke the door opened and a young lady entered the room.

relationships.description: Holmes leads the discussion, arguing that logic should be prioritized over sensationalism in case records.
relationships.context: 'At the same time,' he remarked after a pause... 'you can hardly be open to a charge of sensationalism...'

relationships.description: Watson defends his writing style against accusations of sensationalism while acknowledging the charge.

properties.contribution: defends his records and acknowledges the charge

relationships.context: 'And yet,' said I, smiling, 'I cannot quite hold myself absolved from the charge of sensationalism which has been urged against my records.'

relationships.description: Violet Hunter visits Baker Street to consult Holmes regarding her employment dilemma.
relationships.context: I shall call at half-past ten to-morrow if I do not inconvenience you.

relationships.description: Holmes lives and works in Baker Street, where he conducts his investigations and receives clients.
relationships.context: we sat after breakfast on either side of a cheery fire in the old room at Baker Street.

relationships.description: Watson lives and works alongside Holmes in Baker Street, sharing the residence.

relationships.description: Violet Hunter resides at Montague Place, as indicated by the address on her letter to Holmes.
relationships.context: It was dated from Montague Place upon the preceding evening, and ran thus:

relationships.description: Colonel Munro received an appointment in Halifax, Nova Scotia, and relocated there with his children.
relationships.context: the colonel received an appointment at Halifax, in Nova Scotia, and took his children over to America with him

relationships.description: Violet Hunter frequently visits the West End to check for governess positions at Westaway's agency.
relationships.context: There is a well-known agency for governesses in the West End called Westaway's, and there I used to call about once a week

relationships.description: Violet Hunter regularly visits Westaway's agency to seek employment as a governess.
relationships.context: There is a well-known agency for governesses in the West End called Westaway's, and there I used to call about once a week

relationships.description: Miss Stoper manages Westaway's agency, handling client consultations and employment ledgers.
relationships.context: Westaway was the name of the founder of the business, but it is really managed by Miss Stoper.

relationships.description: Holmes reads the Daily Telegraph to scan advertisement columns for potential cases.
relationships.context: tossing aside the advertisement sheet of the Daily Telegraph

relationships.description: Holmes smokes a long cherry-wood pipe during disputatious or argumentative moods.
relationships.context: lighting with it the long cherry-wood pipe which was wont to replace his clay when he was in a disputatious rather than a meditative mood

relationships.description: Holmes receives and examines a crumpled letter from Violet Hunter regarding her employment dilemma.
relationships.context: He tossed a crumpled letter across to me.

relationships.description: Holmes previously investigated the Blue Carbuncle, which evolved from a trivial whim into a serious case.
relationships.context: You remember that the affair of the blue carbuncle, which appeared to be a mere whim at first, developed into a serious investigation.

relationships.description: Holmes gives advice to young ladies from boarding-schools, representing their interests in his declining practice.
relationships.context: As to my own little practice, it seems to be degenerating into an agency for recovering lost lead pencils and giving advice to young ladies from boarding-schools.

relationships.description: Holmes considers logic and deduction his special province and the core of his professional art.
relationships.context: those faculties of deduction and of logical synthesis which I have made my special province.

relationships.description: Holmes loves art for its own sake, finding pleasure in its least important and lowliest manifestations.
relationships.context: to the man who loves art for its own sake,' remarked Sherlock Holmes

relationships.description: Watson's case records are accused of expressing sensationalism rather than strict logical reasoning.
relationships.context: I cannot quite hold myself absolved from the charge of sensationalism which has been urged against my records.

relationships.description: Holmes actively avoids sensationalism in his cases, preferring logical deduction over trivial or sensational outcomes.
relationships.context: But in avoiding the sensational, I fear that you may have bordered on the trivial.

relationships.description: Holmes considers his current state of work to be at a 'zero-point', marking a perceived low in his career.
relationships.context: This note I had this morning marks my zero-point, I fancy.
